title: UPDATE, DELETE & Returning

glossary.definition: No longer needed, useful, or relevant, usually because it has been replaced by something newer.
Doing something accidentally or without intending to, like mistakenly deleting the wrong data.

overview.what: UPDATE modifies existing rows in a table, and DELETE removes them entirely. Both use the WHERE clause to target specific rows. Similar to INSERT, both commands support a RETURNING clause to immediately fetch the modified or removed data, providing instant confirmation of the operation's effect.
overview.why: Data changes over time. You need UPDATE to correct or evolve data and DELETE to remove obsolete records safely. RETURNING helps verify what was actually affected.

why_learning_this: You will know how to modify or remove precisely the data you intend.
You can chain RETURNING with application logic to log what changed without extra queries.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting the WHERE clause
common_mistakes.explanation: Running UPDATE or DELETE without WHERE affects EVERY row in the table, which can destroy production data. Always double-check your WHERE conditions.

common_mistakes.title: Incorrect WHERE condition
common_mistakes.explanation: Using a condition that is too broad, inadvertently updating or deleting more rows than intended.

recall_questions.q: What happens if you omit the WHERE clause in an UPDATE statement?
recall_questions.answer: The database will apply the update to every single row in the table.

understanding_checks.query: UPDATE products SET price = 9.99 WHERE id = 6 RETURNING id, price;
understanding_checks.question: What rows does this query return?
understanding_checks.why: It updates the Notebook (id 6) price from 4.99 to 9.99 and returns the new state.

understanding_checks.query: DELETE FROM customers WHERE id = 8 RETURNING name;
understanding_checks.question: What rows does this query return?
understanding_checks.why: It deletes the row for Hana (id 8) and returns the name of the removed row.

query_walkthrough.query: UPDATE orders SET status = 'cancelled' WHERE id = 3 RETURNING id, status;
query_walkthrough.focus: Filtering down to order id 3, applying the status modification, and returning the updated state.

practice_tasks.title: Update product price
practice_tasks.prompt: Given this UPDATE statement, modify it to increase the price of the 'Keyboard' (id 1) to 59.99, and return the new price.
practice_tasks.solution:
UPDATE products
SET price = 59.99
WHERE id = 1
RETURNING price;

challenge.title: Delete and Return
challenge.prompt: Write a query to delete all orders that are currently 'pending' (you can find the status in the orders table) and return their IDs.
challenge.solution: DELETE FROM orders WHERE status = 'pending' RETURNING id;
